dialogue.utterance: Please respond to Unit 3 for a male with chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex from rescue squad. What's wrong today?
I'm having chest pain-second time this week, started about 30 minutes ago.
Are you conscious and aware of what's going on?
Yes, I'm fully aware.
Could you tell me the day and time?
It's July 11th. I'm not sure of the time. I know it's this week.
You're alert and oriented to time and place. That's good.
I see your airway is clear and not blocked. You're breathing comfortably on your own.
Your breathing is at a normal rate and not labored, about 16 breaths a minute.
You look well perfused, with no blue tinge and your skin is warm and dry.
You mentioned chest pain-can you tell us more about how it feels?
It's a feeling of pressure, like something is sitting on my chest and not letting up.
You have severe kidney disease and high blood pressure. Any other diagnoses?
Yes, I have COPD, asthma, a history of stroke, and GERD. I also have constipation and swelling in my legs.
You mentioned missing dialysis. Is that true?
I usually go Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. I missed Monday's session.
What medications do you currently use daily?
I'm on Labetalol, Lipitor, Albuterol, Nexium, oxygen, and also take other medications like trazodone, roxicodone, and norfloxacin.
All known drug allergies or reactions in the past?
I'm allergic to Tylenol and Kayexalate.
You went to VCU on Tuesday for chest pain and received emergency dialysis. Are you having the same symptoms now?
Yes, everything feels similar. I had high potassium then and I feel like it's high now.
Did any medicine help the pain?
I took 324 mg of aspirin and two nitroglycerin tabs, but there's been no change at all.
Your blood pressure is 201 over 108. That's quite high.
Heart rate is 81 and regular, no issues so far.
Your breathing is unlabored at 16 per minute.
Oxygen level is at 94% while breathing room air.
Blood glucose results are not available.
I placed you on a 4-lead ECG monitor. It'll help us track any heart rhythm changes in real time.
Preliminary findings on the 12-lead ECG include first-degree AV block, left atrial enlargement, and peaked T-waves with low QRS voltages.
So far, we're not seeing ST segment elevation or any acute infarction signs.
When did you first notice the chest discomfort today?
Around 30 minutes ago, and it's been pretty constant since then.
You described it as pressure. Do you feel any sharp or stabbing sensations at all?
It's all pressure and I don't feel like it's moving or going away with any movement.
Does it go beyond the chest and shoulders to any other part of your body?
No, it stays in my chest and upper body.
Does it change with any activity or stay the same no matter what you do?
It stays constant, nothing seems to make it worse or better.
If you rate your pain from zero to 10, how bad does it feel?
I rate it a 7.
No rashes, lesions, or other skin changes. Skin warmth, dryness, and color look normal.
Your head is normal with symmetrical eyes. You're alert and orientated to four areas.
No drainage or foreign bodies noted in ears, nose, or mouth.
Your chest expands evenly and both lungs have equally clear and normal breath sounds.
Your belly is soft and not tender. No digestion issues reported.
All limbs have strong pulses and normal sensation. No notable swelling or bruising.
Your heart rate has now increased to 129 with same regular rhythm.
Your blood pressure is now at 215 over 100.
Your oxygen saturation is stable at 96%.
Based on your condition and findings, we're following protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac chest pain.
I'd rather go to Retreat. They did dialysis on me before and I feel more confident there.
Retreat is an option, but VCU has a cardiologist and cardiac care in the ED. They might be better prepared if needed.
You're stable for the time being, but we will keep monitoring you closely in transport.
Your latest vitals are 215/100, 129 pulse, 96% oxygen.
We will follow your choice to go to Retreat today. But it's very important you understand the risks.
Be aware, you're declining IV access and any other interventions we proposed for now.
You understood the circumstances and made an informed decision. We respect your choice.
We'll transfer you to the stretcher now and use straps to ensure you're safe during transport.
Breathing rate is still 16, no signs of breathing difficulty.
Continuing with 2L oxygen. You remain well-oxygenated.
Pain remains at a 7. Let us know if it increases or changes.
BP is 215/100, 129 heart rate, and 96% oxygen still.
Blood pressure is high, but you have no symptoms suggesting stroke.
You are securely placed on the stretcher and fastened for transport.
We've confirmed the care and transport plan with you and everything is understood.
Stretcher is now secured and we're ready to move.
You're still alert and being closely monitored with continuous EKG tracking.
Your entire history, meds used, and ECG results will be passed to the hospital team.
Your mental status is stable with a GCS of 15.
Notes include your dialysis issues, kidney disease, and prior stroke because of CVA/infarction.
Transport is starting, everything is in place as discussed with you earlier.
You understand the situation and continue to make your own decisions.
You're still fully alert and well-answered our questions. GCS is 15.
Vital signs and oxygen remain normal. Nothing impacted the status during movement.
Strong peripheral pulses are still noted in all limbs.
You mentioned needing assistance with personal care. We'll note that for the hospital team.
No deformity, angulation, wounds, or abrasions observed in extremities.
No nuchal rigidity or neck masses. Neck veins are not distended.
You've described it as chronic, persistent discomfort, but it recently worsened.
No signs of obstruction or drainage from ears or nostrils.
I see no evidence of bruising, bite marks, or other skin lesions.
No masses or asymmetry noticed on the chest when inspected.
No masses or tenderness reported in the abdominal area.
No signs of rectal bleeding or abnormal urination reported.
No signs of recent trauma to the face or mouth.
No pelvic tenderness or instability noted.
Skin turgor is normal, no signs of dehydration seen.
GCS remains at 15; mental status is unchanged.
No additional interventions needed at this time due to your requests.
Needle stick or IV access was not performed during assessment.
Oxygen saturation remains at 96%.
Patient weighs 93 kg, and transfer is proceeding without complications.
You'll be placed for dialysis again if needed when arriving at Retreat.
Retreat ED has been called and informed of your conditions and preferences.
Your breathing has remained stable, and there's no increase in work of breathing.
Retreat is within the appropriate range and has resources for patients on dialysis.
Your dialysis dependence, kidney issues, and recent stroke must be understood.
No new rashes, wounds, or bruising have appeared during transport preparation.
Movement of the stretcher has been secured and is ready for transport.
You're still alert and able to interact; this is important for transport safety.
